coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent silicon quantum dots decorated silica microspheres for selective detection and removal of Au3+ and subsequent catalytic application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lu-Shuang
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive detection and efficient removal of Au3+ are important for environmental monitoring and remediation. In this study, silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) were prepared with 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane as the silicon source and boric acid as the doping substance to enhance fluorescence quantum yield. The obtained SiQDs possessed fluorescence sensing ability of Au3+ and were successfully decorated on the mesoporous silica microspheres (SiO2), giving SiO2-SiQDs. Owing to the high affinity for Au3+ and the relatively large surface area, the SiO2-SiQDs exhibited not only selective fluorescence detection of Au3+ in water matrix with a relatively low limit of detection (13.67μgL−1), but also efficient adsorption of Au3+ with a maximal adsorption capacity of 530.7mgg−1. Besides, the Au3+ adsorbed on the SiO2-SiQDs could be in-situ reduced into evenly dispersed small-size gold nanoparticles, showing high catalytic efficiency for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol. The remarkable performance of SiO2-SiQDs on detection, adsorption and reusability demonstrated its multi-purposed applicabilities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Au3+
Fluorescence sensor
Adsorption
Catalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Au3+ by SiO2-SiQDs
Physical and optical properties of SiQDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of SiQDs. Insets in (a): high-resolution TEM image of SiQDs. (b) Size distribution of SiQDs. (c) XRD pattern of SiQDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of SiQDs.
Full-scan survey of SiQDs (a) and SiO2-SiQDs (b).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of SiQDs. Inset in (a): photographs of SiQDs under visible light (left) and UV light of 365nm (right). (b) Excitation spectrum (Ex) of SiQDs at emission wavelength of 440nm and fluorescence spectra of SiQDs with excitation wavelength from 320nm to 400nm by an increment of 10nm. (c) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs. (d) Effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs. (e) Photostability of SiQDs.

TEM images of (a) SiO2 and (b) SiO2-SiQDs. (c) Fluorescence microscopic image of SiO2-SiQDs. (d) TEM image of SiO2-SiQDs-AuNPs. (e) Excitation spectrum (Ex) of SiO2-SiQDs at emission wavelength of 440nm and fluorescence spectra of SiO2-SiQDs with excitation wavelength from 320nm to 400nm by an increment of 10nm. (f) Conductance titration curves of SiO2 and SiO2-SiQDs.

Influence of (a) pH and (b) reaction time on F0/F with Au3+ concentration of 9.85mgL−1. (c) Selectivity and coexisting effect of SiO2-SiQDs for Au3+ detection. (d) Fluorescence spectra of the SiO2-SiQDs suspension with various concentrations of Au3+. (e) The linear plot of F0/F versus the concentration of Au3+ in different water matrixes. (F and F0 referred to the fluorescence intensities of SiO2-SiQDs in the presence and absence of Au3+ or other ions in the sensing system, respectively.).

(a) The effect of pH on the adsorption amount of Au3+ with the initial concentration of 50mgL−1. (b) The adsorption amount of Au3+ on SiO2-SiQDs against the contact time with the initial Au3+ concentration of 100mgL−1. (c) Adsorption isotherm obtained by measuring the adsorption amount of Au3+ by SiO2-SiQDs at different initial concentrations at 25°C. (d) Competitive adsorption of Au3+ on SiO2-SiQDs (q and q0 are the adsorption amount of Au3+ with and without the co-existence of other ions).

(a) The absorbance spectra of the catalytic solution after different time intervals. (b) The c/c0 and ln(c/c0) versus reaction time for the catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol by SiO2-SiQDs-AuNPs in the presence of NaBH4, where c0 is the initial concentration of 4-nitrophenol and c is the concentration of 4-nitrophenol at reaction time t.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent silicon quantum dots decorated silica microspheres for selective detection and removal of Au3+ and subsequent catalytic application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education, School of Life and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education
School of Life and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education, School of Life and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive detection and efficient removal of Au3+ are important for environmental monitoring and remediation. In this study, silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) were prepared with 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane as the silicon source and boric acid as the doping substance to enhance fluorescence quantum yield. The obtained SiQDs possessed fluorescence sensing ability of Au3+ and were successfully decorated on the mesoporous silica microspheres (SiO2), giving SiO2-SiQDs. Owing to the high affinity for Au3+ and the relatively large surface area, the SiO2-SiQDs exhibited not only selective fluorescence detection of Au3+ in water matrix with a relatively low limit of detection (13.67μgL−1), but also efficient adsorption of Au3+ with a maximal adsorption capacity of 530.7mgg−1. Besides, the Au3+ adsorbed on the SiO2-SiQDs could be in-situ reduced into evenly dispersed small-size gold nanoparticles, showing high catalytic efficiency for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol. The remarkable performance of SiO2-SiQDs on detection, adsorption and reusability demonstrated its multi-purposed applicabilities.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold is a kind of precious noble metals and has been used in extensive fields on account of its specific physical and chemical properties [1]. Hence, a large amount of gold-containing waste is produced and discharged into the environment inevitably. Au3+ was claimed to have negative effects on the human beings and ecosystem. For example, Au3+ may bind tightly to DNA or enzyme thus damaging the liver, kidney and peripheral nervous system [2]. In addition, Au3+ can be absorbed and accumulated by plants thus inhibiting their growth [1]. Therefore, detection and removal of Au3+ are of high concern for environmental monitoring and remediation.

For the detection of Au3+, the instrumental methods are commonly applied, like atomic absorption spectroscopy, atomic fluorescence spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy [1,2]. As these techniques often rely on complicated sample pretreatment processes and expensive equipments, the convenient and simple detection method is desired. Fluorescence sensor could be an alternative choice, in light of its simple and fast detection operation. Some Au3+ fluorescence probes were proposed based on rhodamine, boron dipyrromethene, fluorescein and naphthalimide in addition to some newly designed fluorophores [3]. Though these probes were successfully developed, some expensive specific reagents and complex synthesis procedures were still involved. Silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), as a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials, have been claimed to have advantages of chemical inertness, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, good fluorescence performance, high photostability, and especially their superiority to traditional toxic semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent dyes in terms of environmental-friendliness [4–7]. Therefore, many efforts were devoted in the last few years to enhance the fluorescence performance of SiQDs and diversify their applications [4–6,8,9]. A facile fluorescent probe for the detection of hydroquinone was developed by Liu et al. using novel SiQDs [6]. Zhang et al. developed a chemiluminescence method for the detection of dopamine by the combination of SiQDs, luminol and K2S2O8 [10]. Dong et al. proposed a biosensor for the detection of target DNA based on the electrogenerated chemiluminescence-resonance energy transfer process between SiQDs and gold nanoparticles [11]. But so far, to the best of our knowledge, there was no report on fluorescent sensing of Au3+ by SiQDs.

Apart from the detection of residual Au3+ in the environment, removal of it was highly desired for environmental remediation. Adsorption was one of the most extensively employed methods, owing to its advantages of cost-effectiveness, simple operation and efficient removal efficiency [12]. It is a hot research topic to develop the adsorbent with a high adsorption capacity of Au3+ [12–14].

Recently, some attempts were made to obtain dual detection and adsorption of toxic analytes by using binding sites-containing fluorophores modified solid structures [15–17]. Additionally, carbon dots embedded zincone microspheres were proposed for luminescent detection and removal of dichromate anions [18]. The main advantage of the solid sensor was that it could be easily recovered in heterogeneous solid-liquid phases. Also, the system may allow monitoring of the removal effect without other external devices [19]. From an economical respect, one material realizing multiple functions was highly appreciated.

The simultaneous detection and removal of Au3+ by one material would be appealing for energy and cost saving; from the respect of sustainability, it would be more appreciated if the Au3+ could be recycled or reused. In this study, highly fluorescent boron-doped SiQDs was deliberately designed and prepared. 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTMS) played the triple roles, the silicon source, the nitrogen source with high affinity for Au3+, and the residual siloxane groups facilitating SiQDs’ immobilization onto the silica microspheres (SiO2). The doping of boron dramatically enhanced the fluorescence of the SiQDs. The SiQDs were immobilized onto SiO2, obtaining the SiO2-SiQDs, achieving simultaneous detection and adsorption of Au3+ with high sensitivity and large adsorption capacity. Additionally, the adsorbed Au3+ could be in situ reduced by NaBH4 to form the uniform small-size gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with high catalytic ability.

APTMS was obtained from Hubei Wuhan University Silicone New Material Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). HAuCl4·3H2O was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Boric acid, hydrochloric acid (HCl), 4-nitrophenol, NaBH4, NaOH and other metal ion salts used in this study were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was produced by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China). The mesoporous SiO2 was prepared according to the previous study [20].

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were captured by a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). The fluorescence images were taken by an Olympus CKX53 microscope (Olympus, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected on an XRD-6100 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in the range of 10° to 80° (2θ). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) spectra were recorded by an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA) in the wavenumber range of 400cm−1 to 4000cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a TG 209F1 Libra thermal analyzer (Netzsch, Germany) at a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. The nitrogen adsorption isotherms were obtained on a TriStar II surface area analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). The surface area was calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, and the pore diameter and pore volume were calculated by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model. UV–vis spectra were recorded by a Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) was conducted by an AA-6880 atomic absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The conductance titration was performed to estimate the amount of nitrogen which could be protonated on the SiO2-SiQDs by a conductivity meter (DDS-307 Lei Ci, Shanghai, China) [21]. The quantum yield (QY) of the SiQDs was determined with quinine sulfate as reference according to the previous report [2].

Briefly, 0.5g boric acid and 2mL APTMS with the molar ratio of ∼1:1 were evenly mixed and transferred into a 25mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave, with heating at 200°C for 6h. After natural cooling down to room temperature, the obtained viscous liquid product was dispersed in 10mL ultrapure water and adjusted to neutral with HCl. The solution was transferred into a dialysis bag (1000 MWCO) and dialyzed against ultrapure water for 12h with renewing ultrapure water every 2h to remove unreacted small molecules. Finally, the SiQDs solution was evaporated, washed with ethanol and dried in an oven to obtain the solid SiQDs.

SiO2 (1.0g) was ultrasonically dispersed evenly in 10mL SiQDs solution (200mgL−1), and heated at 120°C for 8h in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was centrifugated and the solid was washed with water several times to remove the unreacted SiQDs. The obtained SiO2-SiQDs were dried at 80°C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Au3+ by SiO2-SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, the Au3+ aqueous samples were adjusted to a certain pH with HCl or NaOH solution. SiO2-SiQDs (2mg) were uniformly dispersed into the Au3+ solution (4mL) and incubated for the specified time before the fluorescence measurement. The emission spectra were obtained under the excitation wavelength of 360nm, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation and emission wavelengths of 360nm and 440nm, respectively. For the selectivity experiment, SiO2-SiQDs were dispersed into the investigated individual ion aqueous solution (3.94mgL−1, 4mL), including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Zr4+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Hg2+, SO4 2-, Cl−, NO3 −, PO4 3- and AcO−. Furthermore, to assess the coexistence influence, SiO2-SiQDs were dispersed into Au3+ solution (3.94mgL−1, 4mL) spiked with other individual ions separately. The fluorescence intensity of these samples were measured under the same condition as above.

The river water and tap water, which were collected from Yangtze River (Wuhan, China) and our laboratory, respectively, were checked to be free of Au3+. Certain amounts of Au3+ were then added to real waters to obtain the different spiked concentrations. The established sensing method was applied to the spiked waters as the above procedure in triplicate.

In a typical process, SiO2-SiQDs (2mg) were dispersed in Au3+ aqueous solution (4mL) of the certain pH, and shaken on a temperature-controlled rotary shaker with a constant speed of 60rmin−1 at 25°C. After a fixed duration, the suspension was filtered through a 0.22μm membrane and the concentration of Au3+ in the filtrate was analyzed by AAS. The amount of Au3+ adsorbed onto the adsorbents was calculated according to the Eq. (1),
(1) qt=(c0-ct)V/m

where qt is the adsorption amount (mg g−1) at time t, c0 and ct are the concentrations of Au3+ at initial and t time in the solution (mg L−1), V is the volume of Au3+ solution (mL) and m is the weight of the adsorbents (mg).

In order to get the optimum adsorption conditions, the effect of initial pH on the adsorption amount was firstly studied with an initial Au3+ concentration of 50mgL−1 at pH from 2 to 9. The adsorption kinetic experiment was conducted at the initial Au3+ concentration of 100mgL−1 at pH 3; the suspension was filtrated at different time intervals and the concentration of residual Au3+ in aqueous solution was measured by AAS. The adsorption isotherms were investigated by varying the initial Au3+ concentration in the range of 100–400mgL−1 at pH 3, with adsorption time of 6h at 25°C.

As for the evaluation of competitive adsorption of metal ions onto SiO2-SiQDs, different metal ions of 100mgL−1, e.g. Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+ and Ni2+, were introduced into the Au3+ solution (100mgL−1), separately. The adsorption amount of Au3+ was also determined according to Eq. (1).

For regeneration of SiO2-SiQDs, the SiO2-SiQDs-Au3+ were immersed in the aqueous solution of 0.5mol L−1 thiourea and 0.1mol L−1 HCl with shaking for 1h. The solid was separated by centrifugation and washed with deionized water several times till neutral, and dried at 80°C for the next adsorption cycle with the initial Au3+ concentration of 100mgL−1.

Au3+ on SiO2-SiQDs-Au3+ could be further in situ reduced to AuNPs. After adsorption, SiO2-SiQDs-Au3+ were separated and washed with water by centrifugation for several times and then 20mg of them were dispersed in 15mL ultrapure water, with the addition of NaBH4 aqueous solution (0.6mol L−1, 1mL) under magnetic stirring. After 1h, the solid, i.e. SiO2-SiQDs-AuNPs, was collected by centrifugation, thoroughly washed with water, and dried at 80°C for further use.

In a typical catalytic experiment, SiO2-SiQDs-AuNPs (5mg) were dispersed in 50mL of the freshly prepared aqueous solution containing 0.1mmol L−1 4-nitrophenol and 15mmol L−1 NaBH4 with shaking. The absorption spectra with wavelength from 250nm to 500nm and absorption intensity at 400nm were monitored at appropriate time intervals.

Some fluorescent nanoparticles were applied as fluorescent probes for different metal ions in many studies based on the fluorescence quenching via the electron transfer after their binding with ions through the electrostatic interaction and/or the coordination effect [2,22]. The SiQDs also demonstrated their applicability as the fluorescence sensor for metal ions, like chromium (VI) [9]. The functionality of SiQDs determined their affinity for specific ions, which was crucial to the selectivity and sensitivity of the fluorescence sensors. In order to achieve simultaneous sensing and adsorption of Au3+ with enhanced reusability and convenience, SiQDs were additionally preferred to be easily immobilized onto the carrier, i.e. SiO2 herein. Considering SiQDs usually inherited functional groups from their precursors, a nitrogen-containing silane coupling agent, APTMS, was deliberately selected as the silicon source to achieve dual purposes in addition to the silica source: strong binding affinity towards Au3+ and easy immobilization onto SiO2.

The SiQDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. In our preliminary experiment, the QY of the obtained SiQDs (QYSiQDs) was relatively low with sole APTMS as the silicon precursor. To enhance the QY, doping with other elements was attempted; boric acid was demonstrated to be effective for this purpose and introduced into the SiQDs preparation system. The synthetic conditions of SiQDs were explored in detail, including the doping amount of boric acid, the heating temperature and time. As depicted in Fig. S1(a), QYSiQDs increased gradually from 9.4% to 49.6% and then stayed relatively constant along with the increasing molar ratio of boric acid to APTMS, reflecting the essential role of boron to enhance QYSiQDs. As shown in Fig. S1(b), the QYSiQDs gradually increased with the elevated reaction temperature from 140°C to 200°C, indicating the positive influence of the temperature on QYSiQDs, possibly due to the occurrence of the more effective emissive sites on SiQDs under the higher temperature with improved oxidation performance of the hydrothermal system [23]. Furthermore, as reflected in Fig. S1(c), the QYSiQDs reached the maximum at 6h. Prolonging the reaction time even led to the decrease of QYSiQDs, possibly because some fluorescence sites were destroyed after higher carbonization with prolonging reaction time. Based on the above optimization, the optimum synthetic conditions were heating the mixture of 0.5g boric acid and 2mL APTMS at 200°C for 6h. Thus-obtained SiQDs had the QYSiQDs of 52.6%, which was relatively higher compared to the majority of reported works [4,8,9,24,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and optical properties of SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As photographed in Fig. 1 (a), the prepared SiQDs were uniform pseudo spheres and monodispersed in the size range of 8nm–12nm centering at 10nm (Fig. 1(b)). High-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1(a) inset) revealed that the SiQDs exhibited a good crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.33nm, which was further confirmed by XRD pattern with a broad peak centered at 2θ=23° (Fig. 1(c)) [2,23].

As annotated in Fig. 1(d), FTIR spectrum demonstrated that multiple functionalities, e.g. −OH, −COOH, –NH2, etc., were present on the SiQDs which endowed SiQDs with the high hydrophilicity [26–29]. Moreover, XPS was employed to examine the nature of bonds and surface chemical composition of the SiQDs. Fig. 2 (a) shows the full-scan survey of SiQDs in the range of 0−800 eV. Five characteristic peaks at 192 eV, 102 eV, 285 eV, 532 eV and 399 eV were observed, representing B 1s (6.28%), Si 2p (10.87%), C 1s (45.29%), O 1s (25.69%) and N 1s (11.87%), respectively [22,23,27,29]. The detailed peak-fitting analysis is shown in Fig. S2(a-I-e-I). These results confirmed the successful doping of B and the abundant nitrogen-containing groups on the SiQDs surface which are crucial for the Au3+ detection.

The aqueous solution of SiQDs was colorless and transparent under daylight and showed bright blue fluorescence under 365nm UV light (Fig. 3 (a) inset). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of SiQDs revealed two peaks at 270nm and 305nm attributing to π-π* transition of graphite sp2 domain and n-π* transition of C=X (X=O, N), respectively (Fig. 3(a)) [30,31]. Fig. 3(b) displayed the fluorescence emission of the SiQDs solution under diverse excitation wavelengths. By increasing the excitation wavelengths from 320nm to 400nm, the corresponding emission peaks gradually red-shifted from 420nm to 460nm with the first enhanced and then gradually decreased emission intensity, characteristic of the excitation dependent emission property as reported [2,22]. The highest fluorescence was obtained at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 360nm and 440nm, which were fixed in the following detection experiments.

The fluorescence stability of SiQDs was investigated from the aspects of the aqueous pH, ionic strength and photo irradiation [32,33]. The fluorescence intensity of SiQDs remained stable in a wide pH range from 2 to 12, and only slightly decreased (<15%) in strong acidic or basic conditions (Fig. 3(c)), which was possibly caused by protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups of SiQDs [34]. As reflected in Fig. 3(d), the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs decreased mildly with the enhancing ionic strength, only 20% decrease under a relatively high concentration of NaCl (1.0mol L−1), illustrating the high resistance of the SiQDs to ionic strength. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs remained almost unchanged upon continuous irradiation at 360nm for 7200s (Fig. 3(e)), reflecting the excellent photostability. All these appreciated properties would facilitate the applications of the SiQDs in the real environment.

SiQDs were supposed to be modified on the supporting matrix, SiO2, for fluorescence detection and adsorption of Au3+ from water. From TEM images in different magnitudes in Fig. 4 (a, b), the morphology of SiO2 scarcely changed after modification of SiQDs, and SiQDs were distributed in SiO2 matrix. The as-prepared SiO2-SiQDs possessed a weight loss of 15.2% by TGA and showed high blue fluorescence, confirming the successful modification of SiO2 by SiQDs (Fig. 4(c)). No observable SiQDs leakage was found by soaking the SiO2-SiQDs in water for several days. This illustrated the stability of SiO2-SiQDs, as the SiQDs were supposed to be immobilized on SiO2 by forming the covalent bond, Si-O-Si, ascribing to the siloxane nature of APTMS. This feature would be appreciated for the stability and reusability during the following applications. Additionally, the fluorescence spectra of SiO2-SiQDs in Fig. 4(e) were almost the same as those of SiQDs (Fig. 3(b)). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2(b) and Fig. S2(a-II-e-II), the XPS survey spectrum and detailed peak-fitting analysis of SiO2-SiQDs indicate the presence of B 1s (3.14%), Si 2p (20.90%), C 1s (25.35%), O 1s (46.54%) and N 1s (4.07%) peaks, identical to SiQDs. The above results demonstrated that the immobilized SiQDs on SiO2 remained their fluorescence and functionalities, making it possible to construct the fluorescence sensor based on SiO2-SiQDs.

The porous properties and surface areas of SiO2-SiQDs were studied by the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms. As listed in Table 1 , compared to SiO2, the SiO2-SiQDs had decreased surface area and pore volume, but enlarged pore diameter. As discussed above, the size of the prepared SiQDs ranged from 8nm–12nm. When they were anchored to the SiO2, they may block some pores of the SiO2 and be assembled on the surface of the SiO2, forming interspace pores between nanoparticles (SiQDs). Anyway, the relatively large surface area, pore diameter and volume of SiO2-SiQDs guaranteed their availability to Au3+, thus facilitating the subsequent detection and adsorption applications.

The nitrogen-containing groups were probably responsible for the affinity of SiO2-SiQDs for Au3+, affecting the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor and adsorption performance of the adsorbents [35–37]. The nitrogen amount of SiO2-SiQDs which can be protonated was determined by the conductance titration. As depicted in Fig. 4(f), when the SiO2 was titrated, the conductivity gradually decreased and then increased without plateau, like a “v” shape. However, for the SiO2-SiQDs, the conductivity had a wide plateau, demonstrating the abundant nitrogen-containing groups on SiO2-SiQDs.

As discussed above, SiO2-SiQDs retained almost the same properties as the SiQDs. Hence, the fluorescence of the as-prepared SiO2-SiQDs was selectively quenched by Au3+ as expected. To achieve high sensitivity, the effect of sample pH and incubation time were optimized. The quenching efficiency (F0/F) was chosen as a criterion for optimization. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the fluorescence of SiO2-SiQDs could be more efficiently quenched by Au3+ in acidic conditions and the optimum pH was 3.0. As mentioned above, the quenching of the fluorescence may be mainly caused by the binding of Au3+ with the nitrogen-containing functional groups on the SiO2-SiQDs through the electrostatic attraction and the coordination effect, thus leading to non-radiative electron transfer and the surface status change of SiQDs [2,22]. The major species of Au3+ in solutions always existed in the form of a negative anion complex with four ligands (AuClxOH(4-x) −) [14]. So the pH of the solution could only basically change the status of nitrogen-containing groups of SiO2-SiQDs. With the decreasing pH, the nitrogen may be more protonated and positively charged, facilitating the electrostatic attraction [14]. However, at a much higher acidic condition (pH<3), the F0/F dropped evidently, showing that the excess H+ restrained the coordination between nitrogen and Au3+ [14]. At pH 3.0, electrostatic interaction and coordination effect may be compromised to reach the maximum quenching, and this pH was chosen for the subsequent experiments. The incubation time influence is depicted in Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence of SiO2-SiQDs was promptly and intensively quenched within 2min and then tended to be stable after 5min which indicates the fast sensing. So the incubation time was fixed at 5min for subsequent sensing experiments.

As shown in Fig. 5(c), the fluorescence of SiO2-SiQDs was only efficiently quenched by Au3+ and stayed nearly constant to other ions, demonstrating the high selectivity of the SiO2-SiQDs. Additionally, the co-existing ions displayed almost negligible interference to the quenching efficiency. Fig. 5(d) is the fluorescence spectra of SiO2-SiQDs in the presence of Au3+ of different concentrations in ultrapure water. A gradual decrease of fluorescence intensity was observed with the increasing Au3+ concentration. An excellent linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Au3+ was obtained in the range of 0.04mgL−1 to 3.94mgL−1 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9911 as presented in Fig. 5(e). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 13.67μgL−1 by 3.3σ/k (k: slope, σ: standard deviation), which was comparable to other reported nanodots based fluorescence sensors [2,22,23].

Furthermore, the practical applicability of SiO2-SiQDs for detecting Au3+ in real samples was also investigated in the spiked river and tap water. As shown in Fig. 5(e), the sample matrix had an obvious influence on the sensing process, possibly due to the complex nature of real samples. As known, it is difficult to eliminate the sample matrix influence except for appropriate sample pretreatment. However, any sample pretreatment procedure would entail the whole analysis more tedious steps and more analytical uncertainty. Anyway, F0/F and the concentration of Au3+ in these two real water matrixes were also in a good linear relationship (R2 >0.99) (Fig. 5(e)). The LODs of the sensors were calculated to be 40.50μgL−1 and 43.51μgL−1 for river water and tap water, respectively. By matrix-match analysis, the developed sensor had the potential for quantification of Au3+ in real samples.

As characterized in Section 3.3, the SiO2-SiQDs possessed suitable surface area, porosity and functionality, and the adsorption performance of it towards Au3+ was then fully explored. As discussed in Section 3.4, the pH of the system may change the chemical status of the functional moieties on the SiO2-SiQDs thus influencing the adsorption behavior of the adsorbents. Therefore, the adsorption performance of the SiO2-SiQDs and SiO2 towards Au3+ corresponding to the surrounding pH was tested. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the adsorption amount of Au3+ by SiO2-SiQDs was obviously much higher than that of SiO2 in the pH range from 2.0–9.0, demonstrating that the SiQDs facilitated the affinity of the adsorbents towards Au3+. Additionally, the adsorption amount of Au3+ by SiO2-SiQDs reached the maximum at pH of 3.0 which was selected in the following experiment.

The adsorption kinetics was investigated by measuring the adsorption amount of Au3+ at different adsorption time intervals. As presented in Fig. 6(b), the adsorption amount of Au3+ increased extremely fast in the first 10min, and then slowly increased to reach adsorption equilibrium in about 180min. Though the nitrogen-containing groups on SiQDs were mainly responsible for the adsorption, the residual silanols also exhibited some interaction with Au3+. The rapid adsorption at the beginning may be because of the quick interaction of SiQDs with Au3+ attributing to the much higher affinity of nitrogen to Au3+, while the latter slow adsorption may mainly result from the weaker interaction of the residual silanols with Au3+. The adsorption kinetics fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model (Fig. S3(a)), which indicates that the adsorption rate was mainly controlled by chemisorption [21].

The adsorption isotherm data (Fig. 6(c)) fitted well with both Langmuir and Freundlich models with the R2 of 0.9825 and 0.9966, respectively (Fig. S3(b, c)). A good fitting for the Langmuir equation indicates monolayer adsorption of Au3+ on the adsorbents and the predominant chemical sorption; the qmax (adsorption capacity) was calculated to be 530.7mgg−1 at 25°C, indicating that SiO2-SiQDs are promising adsorbents for Au3+. The good fitting of the Freundlich equation means that the adsorption sites were heterogeneous and multiple interactions existed in the adsorption, further supporting the hypothesis that the nitrogen-containing groups and residual silanols rendered multiple sites to interact with Au3+ [21].

Some heavy metal ions, like Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+ and Ni2+, which may often coexist with Au3+ in aqueous solutions, were chosen to investigate the adsorption selectivity. The adsorption competition experiments were carried out by introducing other metal ions separately of the same concentration as Au3+ to the adsorption system. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the adsorption amount of Au3+ on SiO2-SiQDs was suppressed to some extent with the minimum q/q0 of 0.86. This may be caused by the much higher concentration of these ions than them in the selectivity experiment and the nonselective adsorption of metal ions by silanols. Anyway, the adsorption of Au3+ was satisfactory. The SiO2-SiQDs were still considered to be suitable adsorbent candidates for the removal of Au3+ from water.

Regeneration and reusability of the adsorbents are of high concern in practical applications. As we know, many nitrogen-containing groups could be oxidized by Au3+ along with the reduction of Au3+ to gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), which may be unfavorable for the regeneration of the adsorbents [38,39]. However, the SiO2-SiQDs after adsorption of Au3+ with high nitrogen content remained yellow, i.e. the color of Au3+. No obvious pink color which was supposed to be the color of AuNPs was observed on the SiO2-SiQDs, demonstrating no occurrence of AuNPs. This phenomenon may be attributed to the stability of nitrogen in SiQDs protected by the doped boron, thus achieving the good reusability of the adsorbents [40]. The SiO2-SiQDs could be effectively regenerated by the aqueous solution of 0.5mol L−1 thiourea and 0.1mol L−1 HCl with stable adsorption amount of 165.0mgg−1, 164.2mgg−1, 166.3mgg−1, 167.5mgg−1 for first four cycles, respectively.

Besides being regenerated for the next cycle of adsorption, SiO2-SiQDs-Au3+ can also be in-situ transformed into SiO2-SiQDs-gold nanoparticles (SiO2-SiQDs-AuNPs) in the presence of NaBH4 as AuNPs immobilized catalyst. The color of the SiO2-SiQDs-Au3+ rapidly changed from yellow to dark modena upon the addition of NaBH4, indicating the formation of AuNPs. As reflected in Fig. 4(d), the size of AuNPs was very tiny, about 2nm–3nm, thus achieving relatively high specific surface area and providing more active sites during catalysis [41].

The catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of NaBH4 was selected as a model to evaluate the catalytic ability of the SiO2-SiQDs-AuNPs. Fig. 7 (a) shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the reaction solution at different time intervals during the catalytic reaction. After the addition of the catalyst, the characteristic strong absorption peak of 4-nitrophenol at 400nm became weaker gradually with the prolonged time and a new peak appeared at 300nm which was ascribed to the reduction product, 4-aminophenol, demonstrating the successful transformation of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol and the good catalytic activity of the catalyst. This reaction followed pseudo-first-order kinetics in the presence of excessive NaBH4 [42]. A good linear correlation was found between ln(c/c0) and the reaction time (Fig. 7(b)), and the apparent kinetic rate constant (kapp) was calculated to be 1.40×10−2 s−1 in the presence of the SiO2-SiQDs-AuNPs, which was much higher than those of other catalysts with immobilized AuNPs [43]. This high catalytic activity of SiO2-SiQDs-AuNPs may be attributed to the small size and uniform distribution of in-situ synthesized AuNPs on the surface [44,45]. Also the as in-situ synthesized AuNPs without surfactant offered the bare availability of catalytic surface, which provided them with high catalytic activity [44].

A new kind of highly fluorescent silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) was constructed with 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane as the silicon source and boric acid as the doping agent. SiQDs were immobilized on the silica microspheres, obtaining SiO2-SiQDs. Beneficial from the selective affinity and fluorescence response of SiQDs to Au3+ and the well porous structure of SiO2, the SiO2-SiQDs possessed the ability for the sensitive detection and simultaneous removal of Au3+ with high capacity and good reusability. This study provided an idea to design the multifunctional materials for simultaneous detection, removal and recycling usage of Au3+. The potential further real applications are under way.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017TS135) and Starting Research Fund from the Hainan University (No. KYQD(ZR)19106).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2020.01.021.
